did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used two validated Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify melanomas from sequential digital dermoscopy image quartets in high-risk patients, evaluating their diagnostic accuracy compared to dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' and describes a retrospective cross-sectional study involving 59 high-risk patients, two validated CNNs, and 26 dermatologists to evaluate diagnostic accuracy, which constitutes original experimental work producing new results.

title: Monitoring patients at risk for melanoma: May convolutional neural networks replace the strategy of sequential digital dermoscopy?
brief_summary: This retrospective cross-sectional study evaluated whether convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can replace sequential digital dermoscopy (SDD) for detecting melanomas in high-risk patients by classifying lesions based on dynamic changes. The researchers analyzed image quartets from 59 high-risk patients, each containing one melanoma diagnosed via SDD and three nevi, using two validated CNNs and 26 dermatologists. The main outcome was the number of quartets with correct classifications, comparing baseline images against those taken after SDD follow-up. At baseline, CNNs showed low sensitivity (15.3% and 13.6% correct classifications) but high specificity, whereas sensitivity improved significantly after SDD follow-up (44.1% and 49.2%). Dermatologists, informed that each quartet contained a melanoma, correctly classified an average of 24 out of 59 quartets. The results suggest that while CNNs improve with follow-up data, their standalone diagnostic capability at baseline remains limited compared to dermatologists.

lesion_types_studied.details: One melanoma per quartet, diagnosed by dynamic changes during SDD, in 59 high-risk patients.
Three nevi per quartet, totaling 236 lesions in the study dataset.

all_task_types.target: Quartet classification (identifying the single melanoma among three nevi within a set of four sequential dermoscopy images)
all_task_types.details: The study uses two validated Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify image quartets containing one melanoma and three nevi. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. nevi) based on dynamic changes. The primary outcome measured is the number of quartets with correct classifications. Sensitivity and specificity are reported for the classification performance.

datasets_used.name: High-risk patient cohort (University of Heidelberg)
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cross-sectional study dataset consisting of sequential digital dermoscopy (SDD) image quartets from 59 high-risk patients. Each quartet contained one melanoma (diagnosed by dynamic changes during SDD) and three nevi, totaling 236 lesions. The data was collected from the Department of Dermatology, University of Heidelberg, Heidelberg, Germany.

use.testing: The 59 image quartets were used as the test set to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of two validated Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and 26 dermatologists in classifying the melanoma within each quartet.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: number of quartets with correct classifications

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of sensitivity and specificity metrics for the CNNs and the range of correct classifications for dermatologists.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported point estimates with 95% confidence intervals for sensitivity and specificity, and counts of correct classifications, but did not perform statistical comparisons with p-values.

baseline_quartets.notes: At baseline, CNNs showed low sensitivity but high specificity for CNN-1.

post_sdd_follow_up_quartets.notes: Sensitivities improved significantly after SDD follow-up.

dermatologist_evaluation.notes: Dermatologists were informed that each baseline quartet contained one melanoma.

architecture_used.main_model: Two validated Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) (referred to as CNN-1 and CNN-2)

architecture_details.details: The specific architecture names, layers, heads, patch sizes, and parameter counts were not reported in the provided text. The models are described only as 'validated CNNs'.

classification_or_output_head.details: The models output a malignancy score to classify quartets containing one melanoma and three nevi.

conclusions: At baseline, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) demonstrated low sensitivity (15.3% and 13.6% correct classifications) but high specificity for detecting melanomas in high-risk patients.
Sensitivity for melanoma detection improved significantly after sequential digital dermoscopy (SDD) follow-up, with sensitivities reaching 44.1% and 49.2% for the two CNNs.
Dermatologists, when informed that each quartet contained a melanoma, correctly classified an average of 24 out of 59 baseline quartets, outperforming the baseline performance of the CNNs.
The study suggests that while CNNs benefit from follow-up data, their standalone diagnostic capability at baseline remains limited compared to dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study is a retrospective cross-sectional analysis with a very small sample size (59 high-risk patients, 236 lesions), limiting statistical power and generalizability.

The CNNs were 'validated' but not trained on this specific dataset; they were applied as external models to test their ability to detect melanomas diagnosed solely by dynamic changes during SDD, which is a specific clinical challenge.
Baseline sensitivity for CNNs was low (15.3% and 13.6% correct classifications), indicating poor standalone diagnostic capability at baseline compared to dermatologists (24/59 correct).
Dermatologists were biased by being informed that each quartet contained a melanoma, which likely inflated their performance compared to a real-world screening scenario.
Sensitivity improved significantly after SDD follow-up (44.1% and 49.2%), suggesting CNNs benefit from temporal data but remain inferior to dermatologists even with follow-up information.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to compare the performance of CNNs versus dermatologists; only point estimates and confidence intervals were provided.
The study focuses on high-risk patients, so results may not apply to the general population or primary care settings.
The specific architecture details of the CNNs were not reported, only that they were 'validated' models, making reproducibility difficult.
